title: Topic 420: The Psychology of Post-Apocalyptic Games: Survival Fantasy
content:
_The following is a fictitious research paper written for entertainment purposes. The content is a blend of real psychological theories and humorous observations and should not be taken as a serious academic analysis._

# Topic 420: The Psychology of Post-Apocalyptic Games: Survival Fantasy

## Abstract

This paper bravely ventures into the irradiated wasteland of post-apocalyptic video games to ask the question that has plagued scholars for millennia (or at least since the invention of the PlayStation): why do we derive so much pleasure from pretending the world has ended? We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, arguing that our fascination with digital doom is not merely a morbid curiosity, but a deep-seated desire for a simplified existence, a chance to be the hero, and an escape from the mundane horrors of everyday life, such as assembling IKEA furniture. Through a rigorous analysis of popular games and existing literature, we posit that post-apocalyptic survival fantasies are a powerful expression of our innate psychological needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness. This paper will demonstrate that our love for the apocalypse is less about a death wish and more about a wish for a life with fewer spreadsheets and more shotguns.

## Introduction

The modern world is a marvel of convenience. We have food delivered to our doors, instant access to the entirety of human knowledge, and machines that wash our clothes. Yet, for all our technological triumphs, a significant portion of the population chooses to spend their leisure time in digital worlds where the greatest aspiration is to find a can of beans that hasn't gone bad and a safe place to sleep without being eaten by mutants. This paper delves into the curious case of the post-apocalyptic video game enthusiast, a person who, after a long day of navigating the complexities of modern life, unwinds by navigating the irradiated ruins of a fallen civilization.

What is it about the end of the world that we find so appealing? Is it a deep-seated pessimism about the future of humanity, or is it simply that we've always wanted to know what we'd look like with a mohawk and a leather jacket made from recycled tires? We propose that the answer lies not in a desire for destruction, but in a yearning for a world that, in its own brutal way, is simpler, more meaningful, and more heroic than our own. In the post-apocalypse, the trivialities of modern life are stripped away, replaced by the raw, unfiltered experience of survival. There are no quarterly reports to file, no social media feeds to curate, and no need to pretend you've read the latest Booker Prize winner. There is only the immediate, visceral struggle for existence, a struggle that, for many, is more compelling than the quiet desperation of the daily grind.

This paper will explore the psychological drivers behind our fascination with post-apocalyptic survival games, drawing on theories of motivation, escapism, and the human need for meaning. We will examine how these games provide a virtual space for players to fulfill their innate desires for autonomy, competence, and relatedness, and how the act of surviving in a digital wasteland can be a surprisingly therapeutic experience. We will also consider the implications of this phenomenon for our understanding of consumer behavior, and what our love for the apocalypse says about our relationship with the modern world. So, grab your gas mask and your trusty pipe wrench, and let us venture into the heart of the digital abyss. The answers we find there may be more surprising than a two-headed cow.

## Literature Review

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of why we enjoy pretending to be the last sane person in a world gone mad. A growing body of research, nestled between studies on the mating habits of the Patagonian mara and the semiotics of cheese, has begun to explore the psychological pull of video games. While the term "post-apocalyptic survival fantasy" may not yet have its own dedicated journal, the foundational concepts are there, waiting to be unearthed like a pre-war stash of bottle caps.

One of the most illuminating frameworks for understanding our digital diversions is Self-Determination Theory (SDT), a concept so elegantly simple that it's a wonder it wasn't scribbled on a cave wall thousands of years ago. Proposed by Ryan and Deci, SDT posits that humans have three innate psychological needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness [1]. Autonomy is the feeling of being in control of your own destiny, a sensation rarely experienced in the real world, where your destiny is often dictated by the whims of your boss and the availability of parking. Competence is the feeling of being good at something, a feeling that is often elusive in a world where there's always someone who is better at your job, more attractive than you, and has a more impressive sourdough starter. Relatedness is the feeling of being connected to others, a need that is increasingly difficult to satisfy in a world where our primary social interactions are with a delivery driver who you only see through a Ring doorbell.

Video games, and post-apocalyptic survival games in particular, are masterclasses in the art of satisfying these needs. They provide players with a sense of autonomy by offering them a world of choices, from what to scavenge to who to trust. They foster a sense of competence by presenting players with challenges that are difficult but not impossible, and by providing them with the tools to overcome those challenges. And they create a sense of relatedness by allowing players to team up with others to survive the harsh realities of the digital wasteland. As Ryan, Rigby, and Przybylski (2006) argue, the motivational pull of video games is not some dark magic, but a direct consequence of their ability to provide a rich and satisfying psychological experience [2].

Of course, not all scholars are convinced that our obsession with the apocalypse is so wholesome. Some have argued that it is a reflection of our deepest anxieties about the modern world, a kind of digital catharsis for our fears of social collapse, environmental disaster, and the ever-present threat of a new season of a reality TV show. As the BBC's Steven Poole notes, the post-apocalyptic genre often serves as a mirror for our contemporary anxieties, with zombies representing everything from mindless consumerism to the spread of infectious diseases [3]. Others, like Chris Begley, have pointed out the dark side of our apocalyptic fantasies, noting that they can sometimes be a vehicle for a problematic nostalgia for a simpler, more “traditional” time, a time when men were men, women were women, and the biggest threat to your existence was a saber-toothed tiger, not a strongly worded email from HR [4].

And so, the literature presents us with a fascinating dichotomy. On the one hand, post-apocalyptic games are a source of profound psychological satisfaction, a digital playground where we can be the masters of our own fate. On the other hand, they are a reflection of our deepest fears and anxieties, a dark mirror that shows us the world we are afraid of creating. This paper will argue that both of these things can be true, and that it is in the tension between these two poles that the true power of the post-apocalyptic survival fantasy lies.

## Analysis

To truly understand the allure of the digital apocalypse, we must dissect the experience with the precision of a wasteland surgeon operating with a rusty scalpel and a bottle of questionable moonshine. At its core, the post-apocalyptic survival game is a masterfully crafted psychological cocktail, a potent blend of escapism, empowerment, and existential dread, all served in a dirty glass with a garnish of radioactive fallout.

The primary ingredient in this intoxicating brew is, as previously mentioned, the satisfaction of our basic psychological needs. Let us consider the case of a hypothetical player, whom we shall call “Gary.” Gary is a middle-aged accountant who spends his days staring at spreadsheets and his evenings arguing with his Roomba. In the real world, Gary’s autonomy is limited to choosing between a beige or a slightly-less-beige tie. His competence is measured in his ability to find a rounding error in a quarterly report. His relatedness is largely confined to a strained relationship with his cat, who may or may not be plotting his demise. 

Now, let us transport Gary to the world of *Fallout 4*. Suddenly, Gary is no longer an accountant, but the Sole Survivor, a pre-war relic in a post-nuclear world. His autonomy is absolute. He can choose to be a noble hero, a ruthless mercenary, or a kleptomaniac with a penchant for collecting traffic cones. His competence is measured in his ability to build a functioning settlement out of scrap metal, to modify a laser rifle to shoot flaming projectiles, and to single-handedly take down a Deathclaw with nothing but a switchblade and a can-do attitude. His relatedness is forged in the crucible of shared adversity, as he builds alliances with a motley crew of companions, from a hard-boiled detective to a Shakespearean robot. In the wasteland, Gary is not just surviving; he is thriving. He is the master of his own destiny, a hero in his own story, a person of consequence. Is it any wonder he finds this more appealing than reconciling accounts receivable?

But the appeal of the post-apocalyptic game goes beyond simple need satisfaction. It also taps into a deep-seated desire for a world that is, for all its dangers, refreshingly simple. The modern world is a bewildering mess of social obligations, career pressures, and an endless stream of information. In the wasteland, the signal-to-noise ratio is much more favorable. The primary concerns are food, water, shelter, and not being eaten by a horde of feral ghouls. This is a world of clear objectives and immediate consequences, a world where your actions have a direct and tangible impact on your survival. This is a world that, in its own brutal way, makes sense.

Furthermore, the post-apocalyptic game provides a safe and controlled environment in which to explore our own mortality. In a world where death is a constant threat, the act of survival becomes a profound and meaningful experience. Every can of Cram, every bottle of Nuka-Cola, every bullet is a small victory, a testament to your resilience and ingenuity. This is not to say that players actively seek out death, but rather that the ever-present possibility of it makes the act of living all the more precious. It is a form of existential thrill-seeking, a way of confronting our deepest fears in a context where the only real consequence is having to reload a saved game.

Finally, we must consider the role of creativity in the post-apocalyptic experience. Games like *Minecraft* and *7 Days to Die* have demonstrated that the apocalypse is not just about survival, but also about rebuilding. Players are given the tools to create their own fortresses, to cultivate their own farms, and to forge their own societies from the ashes of the old world. This is a powerful fantasy, the fantasy of not just surviving the end of the world, but of building a better one in its place. It is a fantasy that speaks to our innate desire to leave our mark on the world, to create something that will outlast us, even if that something is a giant, phallus-shaped tower made of dirt blocks.

## Real-World Examples

Lest this paper be accused of dwelling in the realm of pure theory and hypothetical accountants named Gary, let us now turn our attention to the rich tapestry of human behavior that has been observed in the digital wilds. These are the stories, the legends, the cautionary tales that are whispered around virtual campfires, each one a testament to the strange and wonderful ways in which we act when we think no one is watching, except for the thousands of other players and the occasional sociologist.

One of the most famous examples of emergent social behavior in a post-apocalyptic setting comes from the notoriously brutal game *DayZ*. In a world where the most common interaction between players is a bullet to the back of the head, one player, who went by the moniker “Dr. Wasteland,” chose a different path. He dedicated his in-game life to being a medic, seeking out injured players and offering them medical assistance, often at great personal risk. Dr. Wasteland became a legend, a beacon of hope in a world of despair, a man who, when faced with the choice between “fight” and “flight,” chose “triage.” This is a powerful example of the need for relatedness in action, a demonstration that even in the most hostile of environments, some people will choose to connect and to care, to be the helpers that Mr. Rogers always told us to look for.

On the other end of the behavioral spectrum, we have the curious case of the Great Toilet Paper Famine of *Fallout 76*. Shortly after the game’s release, players discovered that toilet paper, an item with absolutely no practical use in the game, was a surprisingly rare commodity. What followed was a speculative bubble that would have made the Dutch tulip maniacs of the 17th century proud. Players began hoarding toilet paper, trading it for valuable weapons and armor, and establishing themselves as the Charmin-brand barons of the wasteland. This bizarre episode is a hilarious and surprisingly insightful commentary on consumer psychology, a demonstration of how scarcity, even artificial scarcity, can drive us to attribute value to the most mundane of objects. It is a reminder that even after the bombs have fallen, the human instinct to corner the market on a useless commodity remains stubbornly intact.

Then there is the matter of rebuilding. On countless apocalypse-themed *Minecraft* servers, players have come together to create architectural marvels that defy the very notion of a world in ruins. They have built gleaming cities from the ashes of the old world, vast agricultural complexes that could feed a nation, and intricate transportation systems that put the pre-apocalyptic infrastructure to shame. These are not just acts of survival; they are acts of creation, of defiance, of a collective will to not just endure, but to build something better. These digital cathedrals are a testament to the power of human ingenuity and the deep-seated need to impose order on a chaotic world, to create a home in the heart of the abyss.

And, of course, no discussion of real-world examples would be complete without a nod to the “Leeroy Jenkins” of the wasteland, the player who, in the face of overwhelming odds and a carefully laid plan, chooses to charge headfirst into a den of super mutants with nothing but a pool cue and a war cry. This player is not driven by a desire for autonomy, competence, or relatedness. They are driven by a pure, unadulterated love of chaos, a desire to see what happens when you poke a Deathclaw with a stick. They are the jesters of the apocalypse, the agents of entropy, the reason why even the best-laid plans of mice and men often end in a mushroom cloud. They are a reminder that for all our high-minded psychological theories, sometimes, people just want to have fun, and sometimes, fun looks a lot like a glorious, fiery, and entirely preventable death.

## Discussion

What, then, can we learn from our collective obsession with the digital apocalypse? What do these pixelated wastelands reveal about our own, non-pixelated world? The implications, we argue, are as far-reaching as the blast radius of a Fat Man mini-nuke, and they touch upon everything from our understanding of human behavior to the very nature of consumerism.

First and foremost, the popularity of post-apocalyptic games serves as a powerful reminder that the modern world, for all its comforts and conveniences, is not a psychologically perfect environment. The fact that so many people are willing to trade their comfortable lives for a chance to fight for survival in a radioactive hellscape suggests that there is a deep-seated dissatisfaction with the status quo, a yearning for a life that is more authentic, more challenging, and more meaningful. This is not to say that we should all abandon our jobs and start hoarding canned goods, but it does suggest that we might be wise to consider how we can better satisfy our needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness in our everyday lives. Perhaps this means more flexible work arrangements, more opportunities for skill development, or more community-building activities that don't involve a screen. Or perhaps it just means we need more laser rifles in the workplace. The research is still out on that one.

Secondly, the behavior of players in these games provides a fascinating, if somewhat terrifying, window into the world of consumer psychology. The Great Toilet Paper Famine of *Fallout 76* is a perfect example of this. It demonstrates that the principles of supply and demand, of perceived value and conspicuous consumption, are not just features of our modern capitalist society, but are deeply ingrained in the human psyche. Even in a world where the traditional economy has collapsed, we will find new and inventive ways to create status symbols and to engage in the timeless art of ripping each other off. This has profound implications for marketers and advertisers, who, if they are paying attention, should be taking copious notes. The next time there is a shortage of a popular product, they will know that the key to maximizing profits is to lean into the absurdity, to create a sense of artificial scarcity, and to watch as the masses trample each other for a chance to own a piece of the zeitgeist, even if that piece is a roll of two-ply.

Finally, the phenomenon of the post-apocalyptic survival game forces us to confront our own relationship with technology. We live in an age of unprecedented technological advancement, an age where we carry in our pockets devices that are more powerful than the computers that sent humanity to the moon. And yet, we use this incredible technology to simulate a world in which all of that technology has been destroyed. This is a paradox that is as delicious as it is disturbing. It suggests that we have a deeply ambivalent relationship with the very tools that have made our modern lives possible. We are simultaneously in awe of their power and deeply suspicious of their impact on our humanity. The post-apocalyptic game is a way of exploring this ambivalence, of playing out our fears and our fantasies in a safe and controlled environment. It is a way of asking the question, “What have we lost in all this progress?” and of finding the answer in a world where the most advanced piece of technology is a rusty shotgun and a can of dog food.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, our journey through the irradiated landscapes of post-apocalyptic video games has revealed a great deal about the human condition. We have learned that our fascination with the end of the world is not a sign of a collective death wish, but rather a testament to our unyielding desire for a life of meaning, purpose, and the occasional opportunity to wear a cool gas mask. We have seen that the digital apocalypse is a powerful psychological tool, a way of satisfying our innate needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness in a world that often seems determined to thwart them. And we have discovered that even after the bombs have fallen and the zombies have risen, the human spirit, in all its glorious, chaotic, and occasionally idiotic splendor, will not only endure, but will find new and inventive ways to build, to create, and to complain about the lag.

So, the next time you see a friend or loved one gleefully scavenging for scrap metal in a virtual wasteland, do not be alarmed. They are not losing their grip on reality. They are simply engaging in a time-honored human tradition: the art of the survival fantasy. They are exploring what it means to be human in a world that is stripped of all its modern conveniences, and they are finding, much to their delight, that they are more resilient, more resourceful, and more heroic than they ever imagined. And who knows, they might even be learning a few skills that will come in handy when the real apocalypse arrives. At the very least, they’ll know how to properly cook a radroach.
references:
[1] Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). The "what" and "why" of goal pursuits: Human needs and the self-determination of behavior. *Psychological Inquiry, 11*(4), 227-268.

[2] Ryan, R. M., Rigby, C. S., & Przybylski, A. K. (2006). The motivational pull of video games: A self-determination theory approach. *Motivation and Emotion, 30*(4), 344-360.

[3] Poole, S. (2017, August 15). Why video games are obsessed with the apocalypse. *BBC Future*. Retrieved from https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20170815-why-video-games-are-obsessed-with-the-apocalypse

[4] Begley, C. (2021). *The next apocalypse: The art and science of survival*. Basic Books.
